data.title: Waymo opens robotaxi service to all drivers in Miami and Orlando
data.description:
• Google’s (GOOG) Waymo robotaxi service is making its service available to all riders in Miami and Orlando, Florida, ending its previous “invite only” model. 
  • The company has also begun testing its service on Miami’s highways, adding the Florida metro area to its growing list of cities with highway operations, including Phoenix, San Francisco, and Los Angeles.
  • “Our generalizable Driver, powered by Waymo’s demonstrably safe AI is what allows us to move confidently from one destination to another. Our progress in Miami and Orlando is a testament to our system’s ability to scale, bringing the future of mobility to more people across more places,” Waymo said on its website.

data.title: SA Asks: Which airlines are most likely to pursue merger deals?
data.description:
On Tuesday, reports emerged that United Airlines (UAL) has been eyeing a merger with rival American Airlines (AAL), just one week after Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy said he saw room in the sector for more M&A activity. 

We asked Seeking Alpha analysts Chris DeMuth Jr. and Dhierin Bechai which airlines they thought might pursue M&A deals in the near future.

Chris DeMuth Jr.: Allegiant's (ALGT) and Sun’s (SNCY) merger should close this summer after it gets final regulatory approvals.  After that, Spirit (FLYYQ) will probably take another try at selling, perhaps to JetBlue (JBLU) or Frontier (ULCC), the two companies that tried to buy it in the past. JetBlue needs to do something but would be better off as a seller than a buyer.  

United (UAL) will probably try to do some sort of asset purchase. Will they succeed in buying all of American (AAL)? Probably not—that would be a heavy lift on the regulatory front.

Dhierin Bechai: United Airlines (UAL) is seemingly interested in a combination with American Airlines (AAL). Given the debt load and the antitrust complexities, I do not believe a combination among the Big 4 or Big 5 would be the most logical. The need for better cost amortization is highest among the smaller airlines and the low-cost carriers. That remains the most logical space for combinations, given that low-cost carriers have already started implementing better cabin segmentation but are now seeing higher fuel costs as an added cost pressure.

Out of the Big 5, Southwest Airlines (LUV) may be best equipped to acquire another airline, but there are no all-Boeing operators that would make sense for their business model, given that Allegiant (ALGT) is already set to acquire Sun Country (SNCY). JetBlue (JBLU) may be a better-suited acquisition candidate for both American (AAL) and United (UAL). 

Government officials are now well-disposed to M&A. This follows the government’s realization that blocking prior mergers and acquisitions did not create a stronger playing field for airlines or consumers. The airlines once set to combine—Frontier (ULCC), Spirit (FLYYQ), and JetBlue (JBLU)—are now facing a tough cost environment, leaving them less competitive. M&A activity today is driven more by necessity than strength.

  • Top Passenger Airline Stocks

data.title: Brunswick expects minimal impact from tariffs on metals
data.description:
Brunswick Corporation (BC) issued an update on its tariff situation on Wednesday. The Illinois-based company said the recent amendments to Section 232 tariffs on U.S. imports of steel, aluminum, and copper are anticipated to have a de-minimis impact on Brunswick's (BC) consolidated financial results. It was noted that Brunswick's (CS) diversified manufacturing footprint is a factor.

Looking ahead, Brunswick (BC) said it will provide additional information on its first-quarter earnings call on April 30.

Shares of Brunswick (BC) were down 2.2% in early afternoon action. The marine recreation stock is up more than 75% over the last 52 weeks.

data.title: First Horizon outlines 3%-7% 2026 revenue growth while targeting 10.5% CET1
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Horizon Corporation (FHN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We started 2026 with strong momentum,” said “we delivered our third straight quarter of 15% or greater adjusted ROTCE in line with our expectations, fueled by strong C&I client growth and relationship-focused client activity across our markets” (Chairman, President & CEO D. Jordan).
  • Management emphasized relationship banking execution and competitive discipline, with CEO Jordan saying, “We’re not playing solitaire. Competition is active, but our associates are protecting our base and winning with exceptional service and value,” and adding, “We expect that discipline, along with healthy C&I demand and the strength of our markets to drive revenue growth as the year progresses” (Chairman, President & CEO Jordan).
  • The quarter’s profitability and balance-sheet strategy were framed by CFO Hope Dmuchowski: “We started 2026 with great momentum, including earnings per share of $0.53,” alongside “net interest income 6% year-over-year,” and “a return on average assets of 1.30%” (Senior EVP & CFO Hope Dmuchowski).

Outlook

  • Management said it “continue[s] to maintain our full year outlook,” and reiterated a revenue framework, with an analyst referencing “the 3% to 7% revenue guide,” as CFO Dmuchowski described outcomes driven by mix across fee income and net interest income rather than a single path (Senior EVP & CFO Dmuchowski).
  • CFO Dmuchowski described deposit-cost dynamics as seasonal and rate-path dependent: “I do think that deposit costs will slightly trend up in Q2 and Q3 if we don’t see a rate cut,” and added that Q2 typically brings “more new-to-bank acquisitions,” noting, “that is in our guidance” (Senior EVP & CFO Dmuchowski).
  • CEO Jordan outlined flexibility around capital levels, stating, “we’re comfortable with the 10.5%,” while also saying, “we have a bias where we believe that we can operate the organization on a lower CET1 ratio… And over time, we will get there” (Chairman, President & CEO Jordan).

Financial Results

  • CFO Dmuchowski reported profitability and earnings metrics including “earnings per share of $0.53,” “adjusted pre-provision net revenue” up “8%” year-over-year, and “adjusted ROTCE of 15.1%” (Senior EVP & CFO Dmuchowski).
  • On net interest performance, CFO Dmuchowski said first-quarter “NII [was] consistent with the fourth quarter absent day count impact,” and “Our margin expanded by 1 basis point,” attributing this to “continued strong performance in managing deposit costs” (Senior EVP & CFO Dmuchowski).
  • Balance-sheet items included period-end deposits “decreased by $1 billion… driven primarily by reductions in brokered deposits,” and period-end loans “increased slightly by $221 million,” while “excluding loans to mortgage companies, our C&I portfolio grew $624 million in the quarter” (Senior EVP & CFO Dmuchowski).
  • Credit and capital were described as stable: “Net charge-offs… $29 million,” with “a provision for credit losses of $15 million,” and “CET1 of 10.53%,” alongside “we bought back approximately $230 million of common shares” and “successfully issued $400 million of Series H preferred stock” (Senior EVP & CFO Dmuchowski).

Q&A

  • Jon Arfstrom, RBC Capital Markets, Research Division: “wondering if you could touch a little bit more the pipelines in C&I and whether or not you’ve seen any impact… from the macro uncertainty?”; Chairman, President & CEO Jordan: “The pipelines and C&I continue to be very, very good… [the Middle East trouble] really has not had a significant downward impact on C&I pipelines,” adding “we’re very optimistic about the outlook for lending growth over the course of this year.”
  • Jon Arfstrom, RBC Capital Markets, Research Division: asked on seasonal warehouse lending and outlook; Senior EVP & CFO Dmuchowski: “we do expect to see a seasonal increase in Q2,” while cautioning “I can’t say what typical is anymore,” and said “We have picked up market share,” adding “one of the biggest upsides to our guidance is if we saw a refi wave… although that’s not built into our outlook today.”
  • Michael Rose, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: asked about rising deposit competition and modeling; Senior EVP & CFO Dmuchowski: “deposit costs will slightly trend up in Q2 and Q3 if we don’t see a rate cut,” and said expected new-to-bank promotions “is in our guidance.”
  • Michael Rose, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: asked for credit update and criticized/classified; Senior EVP & Chief Credit Officer Thomas Hung: “I remain pleased with our very consistent credit performance,” flagged monitoring “consumer discretionary spending… trucking, auto, restaurants,” and said direct private credit exposure is “less than 1% of our loan book.”
  • Jared David Shaw, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: asked whether AI/cost saves are embedded in the “$100 million plus PPNR”; Senior EVP & CFO Dmuchowski: “there is nothing in there about expenses. That is all… revenue,” and described AI/tech as enabling scale “without having to scale the back office.”
  • Casey Haire: asked about NII/NIM drivers and fixed-asset repricing; Senior EVP & CFO Dmuchowski said fee-volatility risk at FHN Financial was visible but “we don’t see any risk to our outlook of our guidance on all revenues,” while Chairman, President & CEO Jordan said “about $6 billion to $7 billion of assets… repriced principally at higher rates.”
  • Anthony Elian, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: asked for updated NIM thinking; Senior EVP & CFO Dmuchowski: “Near term, we do expect to be in the high 3, 4s, within a few basis points of where we’re at right now.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, centered on deposit competition, revenue/NII sensitivity, and capital flexibility, with recurring probing on “what… would get us to the higher end or lower end” of guidance ranges and how buybacks fit near capital targets.
  • Management: slightly positive and controlled, emphasizing momentum and balance across rate scenarios, with CFO Dmuchowski saying “we’re pretty balanced” and CEO Jordan repeatedly returning to discipline and selectivity.
  • Versus last quarter: the current call carried more discussion of macro uncertainty and volatility (Middle East, energy prices, market volatility), while management maintained confidence in delivering within the stated revenue framework and reiterated long-run intent to run with lower CET1 over time.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management’s framing shifted from a Q4 emphasis on year-end capital return and broader 2026 setup to Q1 execution details, with CEO Jordan moving from “optimistic about our ability… in 2026” to “very optimistic about the outlook for lending growth over the course of this year” as pipelines strengthened.
  • The CET1 messaging tightened, moving from a Q4 “near-term CET1 target remains at 10.75%… fluctuating… between 10.5% and 10.75%” to Q1’s “updated our near-term CET1 target to 10.5%” (Senior EVP & CFO Dmuchowski).
  • Analysts’ focus remained consistent on NIM/NII, deposit pricing, capital return, CRE inflection, and mortgage warehouse dynamics, while Q1 added deeper questioning on private credit exposure and NDFI reserve approach.

Risks and Concerns

  • Credit monitoring themes included Chief Credit Officer Hung’s focus on “consumer discretionary spending,” specifically “trucking, auto, restaurants,” and sensitivity to “recent increases in energy prices” (Senior EVP & Chief Credit Officer Hung).
  • Fee and market-sensitive businesses were flagged as volatile near term, with CFO Dmuchowski pointing to conditions where “all but one factor is in red” for FHN Financial at the time of the call, while also stating that did “not” change the full-revenue outlook (Senior EVP & CFO Dmuchowski).
  • Capital-path caution was tied to uncertainty, with CEO Jordan citing “near-term uncertainty about what’s happening with respect to oil prices and what that means to inflation” as a reason “to see a few more cards here” before stepping down capital further (Chairman, President & CEO Jordan).

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a continuation of profitability momentum, anchored by sustained 15%+ adjusted ROTCE, stronger C&I activity, and a relationship-led strategy aimed at the “$100 million plus PPNR” opportunity through deeper cross-sell and pricing discipline rather than cost cuts. Executives acknowledged macro and market-volatility risks—particularly around deposit pricing and fee-sensitive businesses—but reiterated that the company is positioned to navigate multiple rate paths, while sharpening capital targets to 10.5% CET1 and keeping share repurchases opportunistic.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Italy, Ukraine exploring joint military drone production
data.description:
Italy and Ukraine are looking to team up to jointly produce military drones.

“We talked today about how to strengthen our defense cooperation,” Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni told reporters Wednesday following a meeting in Rome with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy, according to Bloomberg. 

“Italy in particular is very interested in developing a joint drone production, a sector in which Ukraine has become a leader in these years," Meloni added.

“War has changed. Now, nobody can feel safe without strong protection from all types of drones,” Zelenskyy told reporters after the meeting.

Meloni also reiterated her support for Ukraine amid its ongoing war with Russia, asserting, "A divided West, a broken Europe, would be a great gift for Russia."

data.title: Netflix Q1 2026 Earnings Preview: Ads and content spend to drive narrative
data.description:
Netflix (NFLX) is set to report first-quarter results on Thursday, April 16, after market close, with expectations pointing to steady revenue growth but a more cautious earnings outlook.

Wall Street expects EPS of $0.77 on revenue of $12.17B, representing growth of 15.5% year over year. While the top line remains solid, estimate revisions suggest some pressure on profitability.

Over the past three months, EPS estimates have seen 15 downward revisions and no upward changes, indicating weakening sentiment around margins. Revenue estimates have been more mixed, with 9 upward and 10 downward revisions.

Investors will be watching whether Netflix (NFLX) highlights content spending and advertising growth as key drivers when it reports earnings, its first results since the failed bid for Warner Bros. Discovery.

The stock slid from early December through late February amid deal-related uncertainty, before staging a notable rebound as investor sentiment improved.

 

 

“The WBD saga is over, and NFLX investors are grateful for it. The focus should now be on whether the streaming giant can maintain its throne on its own, via advertising and live sports,”  said SA analyst Vinay Utham.

“Ad revenue growth and live sports expansion are the primary catalysts; management's $3B FY26 ad revenue target and potential upward revisions are key investor focuses,” Utham added.

YR Research, meanwhile, said “Q1 earnings likely present positive catalysts, with margin improvement and guidance upgrades expected post-Warner Bros. acquisition noise.”

data.title: Morgan Stanley outlines $100B bank reorg and expects NII to build through 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Morgan Stanley (MS) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Morgan Stanley framed Q1 as a markets-and-wealth driven “record quarter with revenues of $20.6 billion and EPS of $3.43,” alongside “return on tangible of 27%,” as CEO Ted Pick highlighted the integrated model in “periods when clients and markets are active” (CEO & Chairman of the Board Ted Pick).
  • Pick pointed to Wealth Management momentum, citing “$118 billion of net new assets and $54 billion of fee-based flows,” and said Investment Banking and Markets produced “a record $10.7 billion in quarterly revenues, inclusive of $5 billion plus in equities,” while “total client assets exceed $9 trillion on the road to $10 trillion plus” (CEO & Chairman of the Board Pick).
  • Pick emphasized capital positioning and regulatory direction, stating, “Our reported CET1 ratio of 15.1% against the capital requirement of 11.8% translates into a capital buffer of over 300 basis points,” and added, “We’re encouraged by this period of enhanced regulatory transparency and balance as we move through rule-making comments toward the finalization of Basel” (CEO & Chairman of the Board Pick).
  • Sharon Yeshaya described record firm performance and efficiency: “The firm produced record revenues of $20.6 billion and record EPS ex DVA of $3.43,” with “efficiency ratio was 65%,” noting “Improved efficiency includes $178 million of severance charges” (Executive VP & CFO Sharon Yeshaya).
  • Strategic updates included closing EquityZen and a digital assets pilot: “We closed the acquisition of EquityZen,” and “We launched our digital asset pilot through our partnership with Zero Hash, enabling select clients to buy and sell several major digital currencies through E*TRADE” (Executive VP & CFO Yeshaya).

Outlook

  • The call did not include firmwide EPS or revenue guidance; management commentary centered on Wealth NII trajectory and activity/pipeline tone.
  • Yeshaya signaled an NII path higher: “Looking ahead, we expect NII to build over the course of the year with a modest increase in the second quarter compared to the first” (Executive VP & CFO Yeshaya).
  • On Investment Banking, Yeshaya said, “Looking ahead to the remainder of the year, Investment Banking pipelines remain steady,” supported by “ongoing strategic activity from both corporates and sponsors” and “increasing needs for strategic capital formation” (Executive VP & CFO Yeshaya).

Financial Results

  • The firm reported Q1 “record revenues of $20.6 billion” and “record EPS ex DVA of $3.43,” with “ROTCE…27.1%” and an “efficiency ratio…65%” (Executive VP & CFO Sharon Yeshaya).
  • Institutional Securities posted “record revenues of $10.7 billion,” with Investment Banking revenues “$2.1 billion,” including “Advisory revenues of $978 million,” “Equity underwriting…$396 million,” and “Fixed income underwriting…$742 million” (Executive VP & CFO Yeshaya).
  • Markets performance was led by Equities at “$5.1 billion” and Fixed Income at “$3.4 billion” (Executive VP & CFO Yeshaya).
  • Wealth Management delivered “record…$8.5 billion” in revenues and “PBT margin of 30.4%,” with “Transactional revenues…$1.1 billion,” “bank lending balances increased $5 billion quarter-over-quarter to $186 billion,” “total period end deposits…$419 billion,” and “net interest income…$2.2 billion” (Executive VP & CFO Yeshaya).
  • Investment Management reported revenues of “$1.5 billion,” “Long-term net flows…$3.3 billion,” and “Total AUM…$1.9 trillion” (Executive VP & CFO Yeshaya).
  • Capital actions and key items included “spot assets…$1.6 trillion,” “standardized CET1 ratio of 15.1%,” “bought back $1.75 billion of common stock,” and “tax rate was 19.6%,” while reiterating “2026 tax rate…between 22% and 23%” (Executive VP & CFO Yeshaya).

Q&A

  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA Securities: Asked about private credit “learning moment” and whether Morgan Stanley is rethinking distribution; CEO Pick responded private credit is “an adolescent moment,” said Wealth alts are “about 5%” with “private credit is 1%,” and added “our exposures are small, are modest,” while “credit should perform during periods when the economy is performing” (CEO Pick).
  • Poonawala, BofA Securities: Asked about the Fed-approved bank reorg; CFO Yeshaya said, “we moved over $100 billion of assets,” which should “allow us to fund assets more effectively,” and framed benefits as building “starting in 2027,” including “more competitive pricing” and “better funding structure” (Executive VP & CFO Yeshaya).
  • Daniel Fannon, Jefferies: Asked what drove Wealth organic growth; CFO Yeshaya said there was “no one single driver,” and highlighted Workplace “greater retention of the assets that vested,” plus “channel migration” toward advice contributing to “over $1 trillion of total assets” in the adviser-led strategy (Executive VP & CFO Yeshaya).
  • Fannon, Jefferies: Asked about cash optimization pressure on NII; CFO Yeshaya said sweeps “have behaved,” described transactional vs. yield-seeking cash, and tied longer-term response to advice and an “on-chain world” where Morgan Stanley can offer products and execution (Executive VP & CFO Yeshaya).
  • Steven Chubak, Wolfe: Asked on Basel and CET1 comfort; CFO Yeshaya said proposed G-SIB would move from “3.5%…to 2.2%,” while Basel could add “RWA inflation,” concluding the combined effect “capital neutral to modestly positive” depending on final rules (Executive VP & CFO Yeshaya).
  • Devin Ryan, Citizens JMP: Asked whether AI is a risk to wealth; CEO Pick said, “AI is our friend,” describing a shift from efficiency to “a productivity phenomenon,” including “co-piloting” for advisers and client agents in equities (CEO Pick).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, pressing on private credit scrutiny, cash/NII durability, Basel capital positioning, and AI/cyber risk (e.g., Michael Mayo asked about “the cyber risk and how that may have increased”) (Michael Mayo, Wells Fargo Securities).
  • Management tone was slightly positive and assertive in prepared remarks, and explanatory in Q&A; Pick used high-conviction phrasing on AI (“AI is our friend”) while emphasizing vigilance around “higher asset prices, tight credit spreads and interest rate path uncertainty” (CEO & Chairman of the Board Ted Pick).
  • Versus Q4 2025, management’s posture shifted from “2026 starts with the tailwinds of constructive fiscal policy and easier monetary policy” to a stronger emphasis on “increased geopolitical uncertainty” and “known unknowns of 2026,” while still describing “measured confidence” (CEO & Chairman of the Board Pick).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 emphasized record quarterly performance (revenue $20.6 billion; EPS $3.43) and a stronger ROTCE (27.1%) vs. Q4 2025 quarterly revenue of $17.9 billion and EPS of $2.68, as cited in the prior call (Executive VP & CFO Sharon Yeshaya, Q4 2025 transcript; Executive VP & CFO Yeshaya, Q1 2026 transcript).
  • Wealth margin commentary shifted from Q4’s reported “31.4%” margin (with DCP impact discussed) to Q1’s “PBT margin of 30.4%” plus a direct reaffirmation that “We reaffirmed our targets at 30%” and a focus on continuing to invest rather than “managed the margin quarter-by-quarter” (Executive VP & CFO Yeshaya).
  • Regulatory discussion evolved from Q4’s broad expectation of “regulatory relief” to Q1’s detailed proposed G-SIB recalibration and explicit desire to “get Basel finalized” (CEO & Chairman of the Board Ted Pick; Executive VP & CFO Yeshaya).

Risks and Concerns

  • Pick cited macro and market risks: “increased geopolitical uncertainty,” “ongoing military conflict in the Middle East,” and the need to remain “vigilant…[with] higher asset prices, tight credit spreads and interest rate path uncertainty” (CEO & Chairman of the Board Ted Pick).
  • Private credit scrutiny was addressed as a market-level “learning moment,” with Pick emphasizing limited firm exposure (private credit “1%” of Wealth client assets; “less than 1%” of Investment Management AUM) and framing expected performance as tied to the economy (CEO & Chairman of the Board Pick).
  • Cyber risk was acknowledged in the context of advanced AI models: Pick said “cyber resiliency is a top priority,” and added, “if the ecosystem risk is likely increasing…then we too need to get our gloves up and take it to another level” (CEO & Chairman of the Board Pick).

Final Takeaway

Management presented Q1 2026 as a record quarter powered by Institutional Securities and sustained Wealth momentum, while highlighting capital strength and regulatory developments around Basel and G-SIB. The firm’s near-term outlook leaned on steady investment banking pipelines and a Wealth NII build “over the course of the year,” alongside strategic moves including the EquityZen acquisition and a Zero Hash-enabled digital assets pilot in E*TRADE. In Q&A, executives positioned private credit as a “learning moment” with limited Morgan Stanley exposure, framed AI as additive to adviser productivity, and pointed to a $100 billion bank reorg as a funding competitiveness lever beginning in 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: BTIG’s Krinsky: ‘Now is the time to buy bonds’
data.description:
Jonathan Krinsky, chief market technician at BTIG, is urging investors to buy bonds now, seeing an “opportunity to buy duration” as he projects 10-year yields (US10Y) will fall back toward the 4% level in the coming weeks.

The strategist also sees the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT), climbing toward $90 over the same timeframe.

 

 

 

The call comes amid a notable disconnect in markets where equities (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI) and oil (CL1:COM), (CO1:COM) have made dramatic moves while bonds have lagged, according to the strategist.

The S&P 500 (SP500) has surged more than 10% since lows ten days ago, and WTI crude oil (CL1:COM) has plummeted 22% from its April 7th highs, yet “bonds have only managed to rally modestly off recent lows,” with the 20+ Year Treasury Bond (TLT) still more than 4% below late February levels, he noted.

Krinsky outlined two scenarios that lead to falling rates. Either continued de-escalation in the Middle East pushes crude lower and risk assets higher, allowing bonds to rally, or prolonged escalation means “higher energy prices should eventually hit the economy, which means growth slows and rates should fall,” he said.

Technical indicators support the bond rally thesis, according to BTIG. The firm noted that 10-year yields (US10Y) are “just starting to roll over as daily momentum flips negative,” while TLT (TLT) is “working through a small base” that could serve as a launchpad for gains.

Seasonally, rates tend to face upward pressure through April but “really start to fall in mid-May,” the note stated.

 

 

 

The massive 21% decline in WTI crude (CL1:COM) from recent highs, which occurred “coincident with the risk-on mood of equities,” has left oil with little support at the 50-day moving average, according to BTIG.

This environment creates ideal conditions for bonds to finally catch up to the moves seen elsewhere in financial markets, the strategist said.

Treasury ETFs: (TLT), (TLH), (IEF), (IEI), (SHY), (SGOV), (SCHO), and (BIL).

Bond ETFs: (AGG), (BND), (VCIT), (MUB), (MBB), (JNK), (LQD), and (HYG).

data.title: Goolsbee sees danger of rising inflation expectations after two price shocks - report
data.description:
After a one-two punch of tariffs and surging oil prices sparked the Iran war, the Federal Reserve may face increasing skepticism from the American public over its ability to bring inflation back to its 2% target, according to the head of the Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago.

"If we’re going to have high oil prices, we may start seeing consumer inflation expectations rising notably. And to have piled this thing on before the tariff inflation went away, that’s a double danger," Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee said in an interview with the Financial Times.

Inflation has remained above the Fed's target for more than four years. "Anytime you’re floating around well above the [inflation] target for an extended period of time, there’s a danger," he said. The two successive price shocks may be "misinterpreted by consumers or businesses as something persistent."

That becomes especially worrisome as consumers' expectations of future inflation are often linked to gasoline prices, according to research from economist Yuriy Gorodnichenko that Goolsbee cited.

The central bank's policymakers generally look through one-time price increases on the basis that they don't create persistent upward pressure on inflation.

While he doesn't vote on the Federal Open Market Committee this year, he said he wants to see evidence that inflation is resuming its path toward the 2% target before he supports interest rate cuts.
